label: Uncomplicated tinea pedis without cellulitis or diabetic foot risk
inclusion_criteria: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated tinea pedis.
No cellulitis, open wound, diabetic-foot pathway, immunocompromised host, nail/scalp/groin pathway, systemic illness, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion_criteria: Cellulitis, abscess, open wound, secondary infection, systemic illness, or rapidly spreading rash.
Diabetes with foot wound/risk, PVD, neuropathy, immunocompromised host, nail/scalp/groin involvement, recurrent/extensive disease, or specialist-directed podiatry/dermatology plan.
Medication selection not supplied by clinician.
default_follow_up: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, dermatology, or podiatry if symptoms are not improving or if this keeps coming back.

source_audit.notes: MedlinePlus and CDC support athlete's-foot/tinea-pedis framing, keeping feet clean and dry, avoiding shared wet exposure, and escalation for spreading infection or non-improvement. Autonomous Phase 1651-1700 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated tinea pedis without cellulitis, open wound, diabetic-foot/PVD/neuropathy risk, immunocompromised host, nail/scalp/groin or recurrent/extensive pathway, systemic illness, or specialist-directed podiatry/dermatology plan.

review.notes: Reviewed as a narrow production ontology phenotype. MedlinePlus and CDC support athlete's-foot/tinea-pedis framing, keeping feet clean and dry, avoiding shared wet exposure, and escalation for spreading infection or non-improvement. Autonomous Phase 1651-1700 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated tinea pedis without cellulitis, open wound, diabetic-foot/PVD/neuropathy risk, immunocompromised host, nail/scalp/groin or recurrent/extensive pathway, systemic illness, or specialist-directed podiatry/dermatology plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated tinea pedis
no cellulitis, secondary infection, diabetic foot, or open foot wound documented
no high-risk host, nail/scalp/groin, recurrent/extensive, systemic illness, or specialist-directed pathway
